key_learnings: The gut-brain axis connects intestinal microbiome health directly to mood regulation via the vagus nerve and serotonin production (90% of serotonin is synthesized in the gut). Deficiencies in omega-3 DHA, B vitamins (B6, B12, folate), zinc, iron, and magnesium are associated with increased depression and anxiety risk. Dietary quality scores predict mental health outcomes independently of socioeconomic factors.
research_findings: Mediterranean diet adherence reduces depression risk by 30–35% in prospective cohort studies. The SMILES trial demonstrated that dietary intervention (Mediterranean diet) produced remission in 32% of depressed patients vs. 8% in social support control. Ultra-processed food consumption is associated with a 22% increased risk of depressive disorder in large meta-analyses.
current_focus_areas: Developing nutritional psychiatry protocols to complement conventional mental health treatment; building dietary risk factor screening tools for depression and anxiety; researching the role of fermented foods and probiotics in mood regulation.
